stories.headline: Aurora teen fatally shot during argument on Lima Street; suspect at large
stories.summary:
A 15-year-old male was shot and killed during a late-night argument in central Aurora on Friday, according to the Aurora Police Department. Officers responded to the 20 block of Lima Street just before 11 p.m. and found the teenager with apparent gunshot wounds. He was transported to a hospital, where he died from his injuries.

Initial investigation indicates the teen and the suspect — who were known to each other — approached from opposite sides of Lima Street and began a brief conversation before an argument escalated into gunfire. The suspect fled on foot in an unknown direction following the shooting and has not been apprehended.

The victim's identity is being withheld pending notification by the Arapahoe County Coroner's Office. Authorities are asking anyone with information to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at (720) 913-7867.

stories.headline: Arapahoe County grand jury indicts Bennett man for first-degree murder in 2023 death of estranged wife
stories.summary:
A grand jury in Arapahoe County has indicted Ronald Lowry, 52, on charges of first-degree murder in the October 2023 death of his estranged wife, Richelle Lowry, at her home in Bennett. Prosecutors allege Lowry killed his wife and staged the scene to appear as a suicide, standing to collect more than $1.3 million if the death was ruled as such. He faces additional charges of stalking, tampering with evidence, and crimes of violence, all alleged to be acts of domestic violence.

Arapahoe County deputies originally responded to a welfare check at Richelle's home and found her dead with a gunshot wound to the head. The couple had been in the final stages of a divorce at the time. Multiple acquaintances told investigators that Richelle had expressed fear of Ronald and had told them that if anything happened to her, he should be looked at.

Cell phone records and location data included in the 55-page indictment show a pattern of Ronald going to Richelle's home when she was absent, and investigators believe he was in the area when her security cameras stopped recording two days before her death. Lowry was booked into the Morgan County Jail on Friday.

stories.headline: Denver Mayor Johnston signs executive order banning ICE from city property, requiring body cameras during enforcement
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston on Thursday signed Executive Order 152, banning Immigration and Customs Enforcement from using city-owned or city-controlled property for enforcement operations and prohibiting city agencies from sharing immigration status information with the Department of Homeland Security without a subpoena, judicial warrant, or court order. Johnston said the order was intended to provide clarity to residents regardless of immigration status, not to provoke the federal government.

The order also requires Denver police officers to detain any federal officer and remove them from an enforcement operation if they are using excessive force against protesters, and mandates that DPD officers have body-worn cameras recording during any such encounter. Spaces traditionally considered sensitive — including schools, libraries, shelters, hospitals, and community centers — are reaffirmed as off-limits for enforcement activity.

Denver Police Chief Ron Thomas said local officers cannot obstruct lawful immigration enforcement but have a statutory obligation to intervene if they witness illegal or excessive force by any law enforcement officer. City Attorney Miko Brown said the order is rooted in long-established legal principles and is constitutional. Johnston was flanked at the signing by Colorado immigrant rights activist Jeanette Vizguerra.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers respond sharply to U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, split along party lines
stories.summary:
Colorado's congressional delegation offered sharply divided reactions Saturday after U.S. and Israeli forces launched major strikes on Iran that reportedly killed hundreds of people. Iran subsequently fired missiles at U.S. military bases in the region, though U.S. Central Command reported no American casualties or combat-related injuries.

Democratic Congressman Jason Crow, a former Army Ranger who serves on the House Intelligence and Armed Services committees, denounced the action as an unauthorized war of choice. Crow called on Congress to return to Washington for a War Powers Resolution vote, arguing that working-class Americans bear the cost of military adventurism driven by Washington elites.

Republican Congressman Gabe Evans, a former U.S. Army officer, called the strikes a necessary step to eliminate Iran's nuclear weapons capabilities and imminent threats to U.S. allies, saying repeated U.S. attempts to negotiate had been met with deception. The War Powers Act gives the president 60 days to commit troops to battle before requiring congressional approval; a White House source confirmed that seven of the eight congressional leaders known as the Gang of Eight were briefed by Pentagon officials before the strikes.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature week in review: bills targeting ICE liability, sports gambling, alcohol fees, and a new Senate appointee
stories.summary:
A Democratic vacancy committee selected former state Rep. Adrienne Benavidez of Commerce City to fill the seat vacated by former Sen. Dafna Michaelson Jenet in Senate District 21, which spans north metro Denver into parts of Adams and Arapahoe counties. Michaelson Jenet resigned earlier this month to lead a foundation focused on combating antisemitism.

Former state Sen. Sonya Jaquez Lewis was sentenced Friday to two years of supervised probation, 150 hours of community service, and a $3,000 fine — waivable with an additional 100 hours of service — following her January conviction on three counts of forgery and one count of attempting to influence a public servant. Jaquez Lewis had submitted falsified letters of support to the Senate Ethics Committee during an investigation into her treatment of aides.

Among other bills advancing this week, House Bill 1271 would impose new fees on alcohol sales to fund addiction prevention and treatment, modeled on how the state taxes gambling revenues. A bipartisan bill, SB26-131, would restrict sports-betting apps from offering proposition bets on individual athlete performances, eliminate credit card deposits, and limit advertising — part of growing legislative concern over gambling addiction. Separately, a bill would allow individuals to sue ICE agents under certain circumstances, setting up a potential legal confrontation with federal authorities.

stories.headline: Wildfire burns 1.5 acres near Chautauqua Park in Boulder; hundreds evacuated, fully contained in five hours
stories.summary:
A wildfire broke out Saturday afternoon near Chautauqua Park in southwest Boulder, prompting the evacuation of hundreds of hikers from the park and surrounding Boulder County Open Space. More than 100 firefighters from nine agencies responded to the BlueBell fire, which was first reported at 12:45 p.m. near the Bluebell Mesa Trail. No injuries were reported.

Boulder Fire and Rescue declared the fire fully contained by 6:30 p.m., having burned approximately 1.5 acres — down from an initial estimate of up to five acres. The state's $26 million Firehawk helicopter made seven water drops of roughly 560 gallons each, which Chief Michael Calderazzo credited with significantly slowing the blaze's spread.

The cause of the fire remains under investigation by the Boulder County Sheriff's Department. Trails at Chautauqua Park, Green Mountain, and Enchanted Mesa remained closed overnight into Sunday. Saturday's fire conditions included mid-60s temperatures and strong winds, though Boulder County was not under a red flag warning.

stories.headline: Fire damages Estes Park hotel, sends one person to hospital
stories.summary:
A two-alarm fire damaged the Expedition Lodge Estes Park on Friday night, prompting a full evacuation of the property at 1701 North Lake Avenue on the east side of town. The fire was reported after 8:30 p.m. and was brought under control by approximately 10 p.m., according to the Estes Valley Fire Protection District.

One person was injured in the blaze and transported to a hospital. The extent of the damage to the lodge and the cause of the fire were not immediately disclosed.

Estes Park, a gateway community to Rocky Mountain National Park, has seen sustained pressure on its lodging and workforce housing in recent years. The loss of any lodging inventory, even temporarily, can affect the town's tourism-dependent economy during the spring travel season.

stories.headline: Denver closes second-warmest, driest winter on record; active spring storm pattern begins
stories.summary:
Denver wrapped up meteorological winter — December through February — as the second warmest in recorded history dating to 1872, according to 9News meteorologists. The city recorded just 0.67 inches of precipitation over the three months, the driest winter since 2008-2009, and only 13.2 inches of snow at Denver International Airport, the least since 2002-2003. Combined with a near-snowless fall, Denver's seasonal snow total of 13.4 inches entering March is the lowest in city history at that point in the season.

Despite the historically dry winter, forecasters say an active spring weather pattern is developing with at least three chances for Front Range precipitation in the first two weeks of March. A storm system expected to arrive Tuesday carries about an 80 percent chance of measurable precipitation north of Interstate 70, with mostly rain at lower elevations and snow in the mountains. A second system forecast for Thursday could bring colder air and better snow potential for Denver and its higher suburbs.

Fire weather remains a concern in the interim. Monday is expected to bring temperatures near 70 degrees with gusty winds and critically dry air, particularly across the southern foothills and nearby plains, before moisture arrives Tuesday.

stories.headline: Estes Park workforce housing program offers renters equity stake, cash rewards for on-time payments
stories.summary:
The Estes Park Housing Authority is operating what officials describe as a first-of-its-kind statewide workforce housing program that rewards renters with cash incentives, property equity accumulation, and improved credit profiles for paying rent on time. The program is designed to help lower-wage workers — many employed in the tourism and service sectors — gain a foothold in a housing market that a 2023 industry report called "broken" for local workers.

Estes Park, a community of roughly 6,000 at the entrance to Rocky Mountain National Park, has faced severe affordability pressure since the pandemic, when a wave of higher-income arrivals blurred the line between attainable and market-rate housing. A Mountain Migration Report found that 70 percent of newcomers in 2021 arrived with annual wages averaging above $150,000, while 60 percent of existing full-time residents earned notably less.

The program is one of several workforce housing initiatives underway across Colorado mountain towns. Breckenridge is pursuing 900 new deed-restricted units, Durango is converting a hotel site into 120 apartments, and a Steamboat Springs billionaire acquired a 104-unit complex and is offering below-market rents. State lawmakers have pointed to such local programs as models as they weigh broader housing affordability legislation this session.

stories.headline: Makar scores twice as Avalanche beat Blackhawks 3-1, extend Central Division lead to seven points
stories.summary:
Cale Makar scored two goals, including an empty-netter, and Gavin Brindley scored the go-ahead goal early in the third period as the Colorado Avalanche defeated the Chicago Blackhawks 3-1 Saturday at Ball Arena. Nathan MacKinnon recorded two assists in a bounce-back performance after Colorado's loss to Minnesota on Thursday. Mackenzie Blackwood made 14 saves for the Avs.

Markar's tying goal came with 8.3 seconds left in the second period on a one-timer off a MacKinnon feed, ending a nine-game goal drought for the Avs' star defenseman. Brindley's game-winner at 7:31 of the third was his first goal since Dec. 11, snapping a long dry spell for the Columbus trade acquisition. Connor Bedard scored on the power play for Chicago, which has lost eight of nine and has managed just 12 goals in those eight losses.

Colorado now holds an NHL-best 87 points and leads the Minnesota Wild by seven points in the Central Division. The Wild have played two more games than the Avalanche. Colorado's next game is Monday night at Los Angeles.

stories.headline: Rockies installing 'Go Zone' hitting approach under new coach Brett Pill to reverse historically bad offense
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies are building their spring training around a new offensive philosophy under first-year hitting coach Brett Pill, who spent six seasons in the Los Angeles Dodgers organization, most recently as minor league hitting coordinator. Pill is emphasizing what he calls the "Go Zone" — a mental and visual cue directing hitters to aggressively attack pitches in the area of the strike zone where they have the highest probability of success — in an effort to dramatically reduce chase rates and empty at-bats.

The approach is a response to one of the worst offensive seasons in franchise history. Colorado set franchise lows last year in batting average (.237), on-base percentage (.293), slugging (.386), hits, and runs in a full 162-game season. The Rockies' 51.0 swing percentage, 31.7 chase percentage, and 29 percent whiff rate were all the highest in the majors, and the team was shut out 18 times, also a franchise record.

Manager Warren Schaeffer said he envisions a "Go Go Rox" offense built on putting the ball in play, speed, and aggressive baserunning rather than home run reliance. Pill said the goal is to tailor data and technology to individual hitters without information overload, enabling players to approach each at-bat with a clear mental framework. The Rockies finished 2025 with a franchise-worst 119 losses.

stories.headline: Report: Broncos have mutual interest in Jets running back Breece Hall if he hits free agency
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have emerged as a potential landing spot for New York Jets running back Breece Hall should he reach free agency, according to a report from Denver Post reporter Luca Evans. A source with direct knowledge told The Post that Hall has significant interest in joining Denver, citing a pre-existing relationship between Hall and Broncos running backs coach Lou Ayeni, who recruited Hall to Iowa State when Ayeni was the Cyclones' running backs coach.

The Jets are widely expected to apply the franchise tag to Hall, which would prevent him from reaching free agency outright. However, if New York uses the $11.3 million transition tag instead, the Broncos could negotiate directly with Hall — though matching the offer would still require Denver to surrender two first-round picks if the Jets choose to match.

The Broncos enter the offseason needing backfield depth after deciding not to tender restricted free agent Jaleel McLaughlin, leaving RJ Harvey as the primary returning back. Pending free agent J.K. Dobbins also faces an uncertain future with the club. Hall is widely considered the top running back available in the 2026 free-agent market.

weather.forecast: Today in Denver it's gonna be overcast. Which means the clouds are just. Sitting there. Not doing anything. They came all the way up here to 5,280 feet, which is already a lot of effort, and then they just decided not to rain, so honestly I respect that — highs of 64, lows of 38, and winds so calm at 7 miles per hour that your hat is gonna be fine and that's a great feeling.
